did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning techniques for skin cancer detection. Specifically, it employs feature extraction methods (ABCD rule, GLCM, HOG) and classifies skin lesions as benign or melanoma using SVM, KNN, and Naïve Bayes classifiers on the ISIC 2017 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving data acquisition from the ISIC 2017 dataset, a proposed methodology for skin lesion detection (including pre-processing, segmentation using GAC, feature extraction via ABCD/GLCM/HOG, and classification using SVM, KNN, and Naïve Bayes), and reports specific experimental results such as 97.8% accuracy and 0.94 AUC. It is not a review or survey of prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection using Machine Learning Techniques
brief_summary: This study proposes an automatic skin cancer detection system to distinguish between benign lesions and malignant melanoma using machine learning techniques. The researchers utilized the ISIC 2017 dataset, comprising 328 benign and 672 melanoma images, which were pre-processed and segmented using Geodesic Active Contours (GAC). Feature extraction was performed using the ABCD rule, Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM), and Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG). The extracted features were classified using Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Naïve Bayes classifiers. The SVM classifier achieved the highest performance with 97.8% accuracy and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.94. The results indicate that the proposed hybrid feature extraction method with SVM outperforms other classifiers for skin lesion classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as benign in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 328 benign images from ISIC 2017. The paper notes that benign tumors are not very dangerous because they grow but do not spread.

Classified as malignant melanoma in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 672 malignant melanoma images from ISIC 2017. The paper describes it as the dangerous type of skin cancer that grows rapidly and spreads.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant melanoma categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between benign skin lesions and malignant melanoma using machine learning classifiers (SVM, KNN, Naïve Bayes) on features extracted via ABCD rule, GLCM, and HOG. The primary task is classification, evidenced by reported metrics such as accuracy (97.8% for SVM), AUC (0.94), sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The study utilized a subset of this dataset comprising benign and malignant melanoma images.

use.training: Used for training the SVM, KNN, and Naïve Bayes classifiers. The dataset was split into 80% for training (after augmentation of benign images) and 20% for testing. 10-fold validation was performed.
use.validation: 10-fold validation was performed on the dataset.
use.testing: Used for testing the classifiers with an 80:20 train-test split.
use.other: The benign class images (328) were augmented by cropping and rotating 45 degrees to overcome degradation and overfitting.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classifiers (SVM, KNN, Naïve Bayes) on the ISIC 2017 dataset.
To divide the dataset into training and testing sets for model evaluation.
To evaluate the performance of the segmentation process (Geodesic Active Contours) compared to existing work.
To evaluate the performance of the classifier models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, specificity, Jaccard Index, and Dice Index.

classification_results.note: Results obtained using 10-fold validation with 80:20 train-test split. Benign images were augmented by cropping and rotating 45 degrees.

Kasmi et al. [6].dataset: EDRA international atlas of dermoscopy (200 images)

ISIC 2017.notes: The SVM classifier with GLCM + HOG features outperformed other classifiers with 97.8% accuracy and 0.94 AUC. Accuracy was observed to be better after augmentation performance. 139 skin lesion images were misclassified by the SVM classifier.

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Naïve Bayes classifiers

architecture_details.segmentation_method: Geodesic Active Contours (GAC) with Otsu thresholding
preprocessing: Hair removal via region filling morphology
Data augmentation (cropping and 45-degree rotation for benign images)

training_details.augmentation: Benign images augmented by cropping and rotating 45 degrees

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal via region filling morphology (inward interpolation)
Data augmentation by cropping and rotating benign images by 45 degrees

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Benign skin lesion images were rotated by 45 degrees.
Benign skin lesion images were cropped.

Applied specifically to the benign class (328 images) to overcome degradation and overfitting, as the benign class had fewer images than the malignant melanoma class (672 images). The augmentation increased the number of benign images.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid feature extraction method using ABCD rule, GLCM, and HOG combined with an SVM classifier achieved the highest performance with 97.8% accuracy and an AUC of 0.94.
The KNN classifier achieved a sensitivity of 86.2% and a specificity of 85%.
The Naïve Bayes classifier achieved an accuracy of 85%.
The Geodesic Active Contours (GAC) segmentation method achieved a Jaccard Index of 0.9 and a Dice Index of 0.82, outperforming the LIN method in Jaccard Index but having a lower Dice Index.
Data augmentation (cropping and 45-degree rotation) was applied to the benign class to overcome class imbalance and prevent overfitting.
The study utilized the ISIC 2017 dataset comprising 328 benign and 672 malignant melanoma images, split into 80% for training and 20% for testing with 10-fold validation.
The proposed method with only the ABCD rule achieved an accuracy of 96%, which was an improvement over previous ABCD-based methods.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant melanoma) and does not address other common skin cancer types such as Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) or Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC).
The dataset (ISIC 2017 subset) exhibits significant class imbalance, with 672 malignant melanoma images compared to only 328 benign images, necessitating data augmentation for the benign class.
Data augmentation was applied exclusively to the benign class (cropping and 45-degree rotation) to overcome class imbalance, which may introduce bias or overfitting risks not fully addressed.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to validate the significance of performance differences between classifiers.

External validation is limited; comparisons are made against prior works using different datasets (e.g., Kasmi et al. used the EDRA international atlas of dermoscopy with 200 images), making direct performance comparison difficult due to dataset heterogeneity.
The SVM classifier misclassified 139 skin lesion images, indicating a non-negligible error rate despite the high reported accuracy of 97.8%.

The study relies on traditional machine learning features (ABCD, GLCM, HOG) rather than deep learning, which may limit its ability to capture complex, high-level semantic features compared to modern CNN-based approaches.

The segmentation method (GAC) achieved a Jaccard Index of 0.9 but a lower Dice Index (0.82) compared to the LIN method (DI 0.85), suggesting potential inconsistencies in segmentation boundary accuracy.
The paper does not specify the 'k' value for the KNN classifier or the specific kernel function used for the SVM, limiting reproducibility.
The proposed method is a proof-of-concept using a small subset of the ISIC 2017 dataset (1000 images total), which may not generalize well to broader clinical populations or diverse skin tones.
